pages.raw_text:
Why should the public stand hat
in hand humbly waiting to learn
whether there is to be war or peace
between the mine workers and the
hard coal trust?


Ask the trust that question, and
1t will by word or manner practic-
ally say that the public has nothing
whatever to do with the matter.


The trusts attitude is that the
mining regions are in the hands of
the trust; that the trust will make
what terms it pleases with its men,
and that it will offer the product of
their work to the public at what
ever terms may accord with trust
desires.


IL the trust used the language of
the saucy boy it would be to tell the
public to "shut up and mind it's own
business"


AND THAT SAME ADVICE
WOULD REALLY BE OF THE
BEST. THE REAL TROUBLE
WITH THE SITUATION is THAT
THE PUBLIC NEGLECTS IT'S
BUSINESS-WHAT REALLY is
IT'S BUSINESS.


And what Is the business of the
public? Not to tell the trust what
wages it shall pay or how many
hours shall constitute days work
for such employees. Both of these
questions and all questions like
them are matters for employer and
employe to agree upon. If the en-
ploye does not wish to sell his la
Dor at the terms offered, he sfthli
be free to reject the offer and re-
frain from working.


Likewise, If the coal which Is pro
duced from the ground belongs to
the trust there Is no warrant In
ethics for the public to break in and
say at what price the trust shall
sell its coal.


Well, then, II the public Is barred
by justice from having any voice
in fixing wages or coal prices where
does 1t come in? Has it no rights


1t certainly has rights and most
potent ones, as witness one of them
All land Is held In private owner
ship, subject to taxation. The trust
